coredata.dc:title: Accelerating H* desorption of hollow Mo2C nanoreactor via in-situ grown carbon dots for electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by ELSEVIER B.V. and Science Press.
coredata.prism:publisher: Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by ELSEVIER B.V. and Science Press.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Mengmeng
Jiang, Yuanyuan
Cao, Zhuwei
Liu, Lulu
Chen, Hong
Ye, Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Molybdenum carbide (Mo2C) is a promising non-noble metal electrocatalyst with electronic structures similar to Pt for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). However, strong H* adsorption at the Mo sites hinders the improvement of HER performance. Here, we synthesized monodisperse hollow Mo2C nanoreactors, in which the carbon dots (CD) were in situ formed onto the surface of Mo2C through carburization reactions. According to finite element simulation and analysis, the CD@Mo2C possesses better mesoscale diffusion properties than Mo2C alone. The optimized CD@Mo2C nanoreactor demonstrates superior HER performance in alkaline electrolyte with a low overpotential of 57 mV at 10 mA cm−2, which is better than most Mo2C-based electrocatalysts. Moreover, CD@Mo2C exhibits excellent electrochemical stability during 240 h, confirmed by operando Raman and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Density functional theory (DFT) calculations show that carbon dots cause the d-band center of CD@Mo2C to shift away from Fermi level, promoting water dissociation and the desorption of H*. This study provides a reasonable strategy towards high-activity Mo-based HER eletrocatalysts by modulating the strength of Mo–H bonds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mo2C nanoreactor
Carbon dots
H* desorption
Electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CD@Mo2C with different carbon content
Preparation and characterization of hollow Mo2C nanoreactors
Mass transfer analysis by finite element simulation

Thank Dr. Qin Wang and Dr. Yang Xu from the Biotechnology Center of Anhui Agricultural University for their help with catalysts characterization in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of the preparation of hollow CD@Mo2C. (b, c) SEM, (d, e) TEM, and (f) HRTEM images of CD@Mo2C-3. (g–i) EDS mapping of Mo and C.
(a) Comparison of the XRD profiles, (b) Raman spectra, (c) the high-resolution XPS of Mo 3d, and (d) FT-IR spectra of as-prepared catalysts.

(a) Polarization curves of CD@Mo2C nanoreactors. (b) Correlation between the overpotential at 10 mA cm−2 and temperature. (c) Tafel slopes, (d) exchange current density, (e) Nyquist plots at −1.17 V (vs. SCE), and (f, g) Bode plots of the as-prepared catalysts. (h) A bar chart of the ECSA. (i) E app and the logarithm of pre-exponential factor A app derived from the intercept during the extraction of E app for as-prepared catalysts at fixed overpotentials using the Arrhenius plots.
(a) The operando XRD profiles, (b) operando Raman spectra of CD@Mo2C-3 in 1.0 M KOH. (c, d) The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Mo 3d and C 1s, (e) electrocatalytic stability of CD@Mo2C-3.
(a–c) The H+ concentration, (d–f) H2 concentration, and (g–i) surface pressure of Mo2C and CD@Mo2C.

(a) TDOS, (b) PDOS, and (c) charge-density distribution of CD@Mo2C (Red represents charge accumulation, blue represents charge loss). (d) Free energy of H* and OH* at different adsorption sites. (e) Gibbs free energy of CD@Mo2C and Mo2C. (f) Gibbs free energy diagrams for H* adsorption on CD@Mo2C and Mo2C. (g) Schematic diagram of the mechanism by which CD@Mo2C enhances HER.

head.ce:title: Accelerating H* desorption of hollow Mo2C nanoreactor via in-situ grown carbon dots for electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemistry, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei 230036, Anhui, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Chemistry, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei 230036, Anhui, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The in-situ grown carbon dots on the surface of hollow Mo2C are beneficial for the dissociation of water and the desorption of H*, resulting in superior electrochemical HER activity.

Molybdenum carbide (Mo2C) is a promising non-noble metal electrocatalyst with electronic structures similar to Pt for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). However, strong H* adsorption at the Mo sites hinders the improvement of HER performance. Here, we synthesized monodisperse hollow Mo2C nanoreactors, in which the carbon dots (CD) were in situ formed onto the surface of Mo2C through carburization reactions. According to finite element simulation and analysis, the CD@Mo2C possesses better mesoscale diffusion properties than Mo2C alone. The optimized CD@Mo2C nanoreactor demonstrates superior HER performance in alkaline electrolyte with a low overpotential of 57 mV at 10 mA cm−2, which is better than most Mo2C-based electrocatalysts. Moreover, CD@Mo2C exhibits excellent electrochemical stability during 240 h, confirmed by operando Raman and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Density functional theory (DFT) calculations show that carbon dots cause the d-band center of CD@Mo2C to shift away from Fermi level, promoting water dissociation and the desorption of H*. This study provides a reasonable strategy towards high-activity Mo-based HER eletrocatalysts by modulating the strength of Mo–H bonds.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to address the environmental pollution and energy crisis caused by using fossil fuels, there is an urgent need to develop clean and renewable energy [1,2]. Hydrogen energy is considered as the best alternative to fossil fuels due to its zero carbon emissions, renewability, and high energy density [3–5]. Electrocatalytic water splitting as a promising hydrogen production technology has been widely studied [6,7]. So far, noble metal-based catalysts, such as Ir, Ru, and Pt, have been widely used in hydrogen production research [8,9]. However, the scarce reserve and high cost limit their widespread application. Therefore, the development of cost-effective and efficient non-noble metal-based electrocatalysts [10–12], including carbides [13–16], sulfides [17,18], oxides [19–22], phosphides [23], and hydroxides [24,25], is crucial for achieving low-cost hydrogen production through water splitting [26].

The commercialization of alkaline electrocatalytic water splitting represents a mature and crucial model for achieving large-scale renewable hydrogen production, holding significant prospects for application. However, the proton activity of non-noble metal-based catalysts in alkaline solutions is significantly lower than acidic electrolytes, resulting in much slower hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) kinetics. The sluggish water dissociation step in alkaline HER greatly reduces the catalytic activity [27–29]. Therefore, the development of efficient catalysts is urgent. Traditional alkaline electrolysis uses high surface area Ni or Ni alloys, unfortunately, these alloys are prone to form hydrides and even become brittle, leading to disintegration [30,31]. Research shows that Mo x C y possesses superior water dissociation ability and offers potential as electrocatalyst for alkaline HER [32–34].

Mo2C possesses an electronic structure similar to Pt and has potential application prospect as a non-noble metal electrocatalyst in energy conversion [35–37]. However, the difficulty of hydrogen desorption from the Mo2C makes it inferior to noble metal-based electrocatalysts in terms of electrocatalytic performance [38–40]. Researchers have explored the use of transition metal modifier to adjust the hydrogen desorption capability, which can enhance the performance of HER [41–43]. However, metallic dopants could dissolve in the electrolyte during the electrocatalytic process, leading to poor stability [44]. Constructing heterogeneous structures with synergistic effects is considered an effective approach to enhance the catalyst performance [36,45,46]. However, these synthetic methods involve multiple steps, which increase complexity of the preparation process. The rational design and synthesis of heterogeneous structures for electrocatalytic HER remain a significant challenge. In situ one-step synthesis of catalysts is an effective method to address this challenge.

Here, we prepared hollow Mo2C nanoreactors via carburization reactions. Carbon dots were in-situ generated on the surface of the Mo2C nanoreactors through the temperature-programmed carburization. The optimized CD@Mo2C nanoreactors exhibit lower overpotential and superior stability in alkaline electrolyte, outperforming most Mo2C-based electrocatalysts. The operando Raman and X-ray diffraction (XRD) tests confirm the structural stability under different applied voltages and cycles. CD@Mo2C shows superior electrochemical stability within 240 h. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations indicate that the introduction of carbon dots causes the d-band center of CD@Mo2C to shift away from Fermi level, reducing the adsorption and dissociation energy barriers of water. Moreover, the acceleration of H* desorption by adjusting the strength of Mo–H bond facilitates the occurrence of the HER process in alkaline medium.

Sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (C19H42BrN), and n-Butanol (C4H10O) were purchased from Macklin Co. D(+)-Glucose anhydrous (C6H12O6), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.

The synthesized hollow MoS2 (further details can be found in the Supplementary Material) was flatly laid and placed in the center of a quartz reactor with an inner diameter of 6 mm. The reactor was gradually heated from room temperature to 800 °C in a CH4/H2 atmosphere (50 mL min−1), with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1, and the temperature was maintained for 3 h in a tube furnace (Anhui Kemi Machinery Technology Co., ltd). After cooling to room temperature in the same gas flow, the sample was passivated at room temperature in a 1% O2/Ar mixed gas for 12 h. The carbonized sample is represented as Mo2C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CD@Mo2C with different carbon content
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared hollow Mo2C was flatly laid and placed in the center of a quartz reactor. The Mo2C was annealed at 700, 750, 800, 850, and 900 °C, respectively, using an Ar gas (50 mL min−1), with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1, and the temperature was maintained for 5 h. The resultant black powder samples were named CD@Mo2C-1, CD@Mo2C-2, CD@Mo2C-3, CD@Mo2C-4, and CD@Mo2C-5, respectively.

The catalysts were characterized using powder XRD over a range of 10°–80° (D8 Advance, Germany, λ = 1.54056 Å). Morphological information were obtained using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, ITEM S-4800, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, ITEM HT-7700, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to determine the elements of the materials. XPS data are collected on a Thermo Scientific Escalab (ESCALAB 250Xi, Massachusetts) with monochromatized Al K α X-ray source. Raman spectra are collected using a LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer (France) with a wavelength of 532 nm. Operando Raman and XRD testing systems are established by combining the Raman spectrometer and X-ray diffractometer, an electrochemical workstation (Chenhua CHI760E), and a three-electrode electrolytic cell [47–50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of hollow Mo2C nanoreactors
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of hollow Mo2C involved one-step carbonization approach. The prepared hollow MoS2 precursor was characterized using FESEM and TEM. FESEM and TEM images reveal that MoS2 consists of uniformly dispersed three-dimensional hollow nanospheres assembled from ultrathin nanosheets (Figs. S1 and S2). Then the prepared hollow MoS2 precursor reacted with CH4 to produce Mo2C.

In order to modify the carbon content, the hollow Mo2C nanoreactors were annealed at various temperatures (Fig. 1 a). Afterwards, the morphological structure of the Mo2C nanoreactors was investigated by FESEM and TEM. The FESEM and TEM images reveal that the CD@Mo2C nanoreactors exhibit a uniform and monodispersed hollow structure after annealing. As the annealing temperature increases, the carbon dots on the surface of the nanoreactors gradually decrease (Fig. 1b–e, Figs. S3 and S4). The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image shows the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of Mo2C (Fig. 1f). In addition, carbon dots can be observed clearly on the two-dimensional Mo2C nanosheets. During the temperature-programmed carburization (TPC) process, the chemical transformation from MoS2 to Mo2C starts at the edge of MoS2 because sulfur is easily attacked by hydrogen atoms, followed by carbide formation [51,52]. The Mo2C is formed by the binding of CH4 to the remaining Mo. During the carbonization process, carbon dots appear on the two-dimensional nanosheets. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping shows that Mo and C are uniformly distributed in the hollow CD@Mo2C-3 nanoreactor (Fig. 1g–i).

The detailed structure of the as-prepared catalysts was investigated using XRD profiles and Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the peaks located at 34.1°, 37.3°, 38.9°, 61.3°, and 74.3° in the XRD profiles of CD@Mo2C correspond to the (100), (002), (101), (110), and (112) crystal planes of hexagonal Mo2C (PDF#35–0787), respectively [35]. Notably, that with the increase in annealing temperature, the carbon peak located at 21.1° gradually decreases and no diffraction peak of carbon was detected in CD@Mo2C-5. In the Raman spectra, the peaks at 814.7 and 990.2 cm−1 correspond to the characteristic vibrations of Mo2C [53]. The feature peaks at 1353.7 and 1587.4 cm−1 belong to the characteristic D and G bands of carbon materials, respectively (Fig. 2b). Additionally, it was observed that the intensities of the D and G bands characteristic peaks decrease with an increase in annealing temperature, which is consistent with the XRD results.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) reveals the chemical state and electronic structure of CD@Mo2C (Fig. 2c and Fig. S5). The survey shows the existence of Mo and C elements (Fig. S5). XPS spectra of Mo 3d can be fitted with six peaks, corresponding to Mo2+, Mo4+, and Mo6+ (Fig. 2c). The peaks of Mo2+ are typical characteristics of Mo2C. The Mo2+ of CD@Mo2C shifts towards higher binding energy with the increase of temperature, indicating that the Mo loses electrons and transfers to carbon dots [45]. The peaks observed at 229.8 and 233.0 eV correspond to Mo4+, and the peaks located at 232.8 and 236.0 eV correspond to Mo6+. These two pairs of peaks correspond to Mo substances that have undergone oxidation during the preparation process. In order to demonstrate the unique role of carbon dots in enhancing the HER activity of Mo2C, we conducted Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) characterization (Fig. 2d). FT-IR results show two bands at 1631 and 3437 cm−1 on the CD@Mo2C sample, which are attributed to the bending vibration of H–OH and hydrogen–bonded water molecules, respectively. In particular, the bending vibration of H–OH on CD@Mo2C-3 red-shifts to 1627 cm−1, indicating the lengthening of the O–H bond of the water molecules adsorbed on CD@Mo2C-3 [54]. Therefore, compared to other prepared catalysts, CD@Mo2C-3 is more favorable for water adsorption and activation.

The HER performance of as-prepared hollow Mo2C nanoreactors with different carbon content was systematically compared in a three-electrode system. We tested the catalytic performance of CD@Mo2C nanoreactors in a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the prepared CD@Mo2C-3 catalyst exhibits superior HER catalytic performance, with an overpotential of 57 mV at 10 mA cm−2, which is lower than CD@Mo2C-1, CD@Mo2C-2, CD@Mo2C-4, and CD@Mo2C-5. From the above, the optimal HER activity of CD@Mo2C-3 could be related to the surface carbon content compared to other synthesized catalysts. CD@Mo2C-3 exhibits stable hydrogen production over a continuous electrocatalytic test in 180 min, achieving a Faradaic efficiency close to 100% (Fig. S6). Compared to the literature reports, CD@Mo2C-3 shows a lower overpotential and better stability than most Mo2C-based catalysts (Table S1). The relationship between overpotential and annealing temperature indicates that an appropriate carbon content can significantly improve HER performance (Fig. 3b).

Generally, the rate-determining step (RDS) can be understood using the Tafel slope, which reflects the electron transfer capability between H* and the catalyst. At room temperature, the calculated Tafel slopes for the Heyrovsky and Volmer steps are 40 and 120 mV dec−1, respectively. The CD@Mo2C-3 exhibits lowest Tafel slope of 96 mV dec−1 compared to 154 mV dec−1 of CD@Mo2C-1, 112 mV dec−1 of CD@Mo2C-2, 108 mV dec−1 of CD@Mo2C-4, and 145 mV dec−1 of CD@Mo2C-5 (Fig. 3c). The results indicate that the RDS for CD@Mo2C-2, CD@Mo2C-3 and CD@Mo2C-4 is the Heyrovsky reaction, whereas for CD@Mo2C-1 and CD@Mo2C-5, the RDS is the Volmer reaction. The exchange current density of the prepared catalyst was extrapolated from the Tafel plot (Fig. 3d). Among them, the exchange current density of CD@Mo2C-3 is significantly higher than other catalysts, indicating that CD@Mo2C-3 only requires a smaller driving force to facilitate the catalytic reaction of the HER process.

The kinetics behavior of the electrode was also investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 3e). All EIS curves exhibit a semicircular shape associated with charge transfer resistance (R ct). Compared to other catalysts, CD@Mo2C-3 shows the lowest R ct, indicating the fastest charge transfer kinetics. The lower phase angle peak of CD@Mo2C-3 demonstrates its excellent kinetic performance (Fig. 3f and g). This indicates that the CD@Mo2C-3 catalyst surface is conducive to the adsorption of reaction intermediates during the HER process. In addition, the double-layer capacitance (C dl) can be calculated by closed cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves to further compare the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the as-prepared catalysts (Fig. 3h and Fig. S7) [36]. The CD@Mo2C-3 exhibits a larger ECSA compared to other catalysts, indicating that CD@Mo2C-3 exposes more catalytic active sites and exhibits stronger catalytic activity than other catalysts.

The reasons for the enhanced catalytic activity were further investigated through temperature-dependent kinetic analysis. LSV curves of CD@Mo2C were obtained at different temperatures (25 to 65 °C) to calculate the activation energy (E app) and pre-exponential factor (A app), which are two important parameters for evaluating the catalytic mechanism. As expected, the HER performance of CD@Mo2C improves with increasing temperature (Fig. S8a–e). The Arrhenius equation was then used to fit the data and calculate the E app and A app values (Fig. S8f). The results show that CD@Mo2C-3 has lower E app and A app values compared to other catalysts, indicating the involvement of more active sites in the HER process (Fig. 3i).

The stability of CD@Mo2C-3 during the electrocatalytic process in a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte was investigated by in-situ XRD (Fig. 4 a). The peaks of Mo2C remain unchanged and no new peaks appeared at different applied voltages. These results indicate superior durability of CD@Mo2C-3. The structural evolution of CD@Mo2C-3 electrode at different voltages was studied using operando Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S9). The vibrational peaks of Mo2C at 814.7 and 990.2 cm−1 are observed clearly before applying voltage (Fig. S10). Operando Raman data were obtained during the catalytic experiments. A two-dimensional mapping image was generated based on the Raman spectra at different voltages (Fig. 4b). The positions of the vibrational peaks of Mo2C remain unchanged, and no new characteristic peaks appear during the constant electrocatalytic process, indicating the stability of the composition of the nanoreactor during the catalytic process.

The surface chemical states of CD@Mo2C-3 after the electrocatalytic reaction were further studied by XPS, TEM, SEM, and EDS mapping (Fig. 4c, d and Figs. S11–S14). Survey reveals the presence of Mo and C elements (Fig. S11). Mo2+, Mo4+, and Mo6+ can be observed in the Mo 3d XPS spectrum (Fig. 4c). Compared to before cycling, there was no change in the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4d). The results indicate that the chemical state of CD@Mo2C-3 remains unchanged during the electrochemical process. SEM and TEM images further confirm the stability of the CD@Mo2C-3 structure. SEM and TEM images after the electrocatalytic reaction are shown in Figs. S12 and S13, respectively. The hollow structure of CD@Mo2C-3 remains unchanged. The results of CD@Mo2C-3 characterization after cycling demonstrate the stability of the structure and composition of CD@Mo2C-3 in the HER process. Additionally, to evaluate the stability of CD@Mo2C-3, we further conducted electrolysis tests by current–time curves. After 240 h of testing at −1.123 V (vs. SCE), CD@Mo2C-3 shows excellent HER durability (Fig. 4e). Under the current density of 100 mA cm−2, the stability can be maintained for 10 h (Fig. S15).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mass transfer analysis by finite element simulation
ce:section.ce:para: Finite element simulation and analysis were further used to simulate the mesoscale mass transfer properties of CD@Mo2C and Mo2C. Based on experimental characterization, models of CD@Mo2C and Mo2C were constructed respectively (Fig. S16). The H+ in the fluid is converted to H2 on the surface of the catalyst. As shown in Fig. 5 (a–c) and Fig. S17, the H+ concentration is significantly lower around CD@Mo2C than around Mo2C. This indicates that H+ is more easily converted into H2 after introducing carbon dots. Conversely, the surface H2 concentration of CD@Mo2C is significantly higher than that of Mo2C (Fig. 5d–f and Fig. S18). The results indicate that the in-situ generation of carbon dots is more conducive to the production of H2, thereby accelerating the catalytic reaction. In addition, the velocity fields of CD@Mo2C and Mo2C in the fluid were simulated (Fig. S19). Due to the same hollow structure, there is no significant change in the flow rate of CD@Mo2C and Mo2C, the surface carbon dots do not affect the flow rate. The pressure distribution of substances in the fluid is another important indicator for evaluating mesoscale mass diffusion. A comparison of pressure fields shows that the pressure of the CD@Mo2C model is greater than that of Mo2C model. The higher pressure in CD@Mo2C promotes sufficient contact between the electrode and electrolyte, thereby facilitating mass transfer at the mesoscale (Fig. 5g–i and Fig. S20).

The influence of surface carbon dots on the HER activity in alkaline electrolytes was investigated by DFT calculations. Models for both CD@Mo2C and Mo2C were constructed, respectively (Fig. S21). Compared to Mo2C, CD@Mo2C exhibits a distinct peak near the Fermi level (Fig. 6 a). The TDOS results indicate that the introduction of carbon dots can enhance the carrier density of the catalyst. The partial density of states (PDOS) reveals the orbital hybridization between Mo 3d and C 2p orbitals in CD@Mo2C, indicating effective optimization of Mo site. The d-band center reveals the interaction between adsorbate-metal to investigate the improvement mechanism for CD@Mo2C. The d-band centers of the active Mo atoms on the surfaces of CD@Mo2C and Mo2C are −0.39 and −0.26 eV, respectively (Fig. 6b). It is found that carbon dots can reduce the antibonding energy, causing the d-band center shift away from the Fermi level, thereby weakening the interaction between the H* and Mo sites [55,56]. The charge-density distribution of CD@Mo2C was simulated as shown in Fig. 6(c). The blue region indicates charge loss and the red region indicates charge accumulation. There is charge loss around Mo and charge accumulation around C, indicating electron redistribution on CD@Mo2C after the introduction of C. The in-situ generated carbon dots facilitate electron transfer from Mo site to C.

The desorption ability of OH* on different structures was also evaluated (Fig. 6d). The lower OH* desorption energy of C is conducive to OH* desorption, thereby reducing the poisoning of active Mo sites and promoting the efficient progress of catalytic reactions. The calculated adsorption and dissociation free energies (Fig. 6e) reveal that the water adsorption barrier for CD@Mo2C is lower than Mo2C, indicating that CD@Mo2C presents superior adsorption and desorption capabilities towards water. The lowest barrier for absorbed H* indicates the fastest dissociation kinetics of CD@Mo2C. This suggests that the carbon dots on CD@Mo2C nanosheets promote the desorption of H on CD@Mo2C (Fig. 6f). These results can explain why CD@Mo2C exhibits better HER performance. The HER mechanism in alkaline electrolytes suggests that this process involves the adsorption and dissociation of water molecules, as shown in Fig. 6(g) for the chemical adsorption model and process of CD@Mo2C. Above all, the DFT results demonstrate that carbon dots on Mo2C nanosheets can enhance the desorption of H* and improve the performance of HER in alkaline electrolytes.

In summary, the hollow CD@Mo2C-3 nanoreactors are prepared through a facile carbonization approach and present remarkable HER performance in alkaline electrolytes. By reducing the carbon content, CD@Mo2C-3 exposes more active sites and significantly enhances HER activity compared to CD@Mo2C-1, CD@Mo2C-2, CD@Mo2C-4, and CD@Mo2C-5. At 10 mA cm−2, the overpotential achieves only 57 mV with remarkable stability up to 240 h, confirmed by operando Raman and XRD. Finite element simulation and analysis reveal that the CD@Mo2C possesses better mesoscale diffusion properties than Mo2C. DFT calculations reveal that the introduction of carbon dots causes the d-band center of CD@Mo2C to shift away from the Fermi level, accelerating water dissociation and the desorption of H*. This work provides new ideas towards the rational design of high-activity Mo-based electrocatalysts for HER. However, maintaining the morphology of the catalyst during the large-scale production is a potential challenge.

Mengmeng Liu: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yuanyuan Jiang: Data curation, Project administration. Zhuwei Cao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Lulu Liu: Writing – review & editing. Hong Chen: Writing – review & editing. Sheng Ye: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22372001), Starting Fund for Scientific Research of High-Level Talents, Anhui Agricultural University (rc382108), Anhui Provincial Key Research and Development Plan (2022e07020037), Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students (X202310364204, S202210364046, X202310364209). Thank Dr. Qin Wang and Dr. Yang Xu from the Biotechnology Center of Anhui Agricultural University for their help with catalysts characterization in this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jechem.2024.04.045.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

S. Ye, W. Shi, Y. Liu, D. Li, H. Yin, H. Chi, Y. Luo, N. Ta, F. Fan, X. Wang, C. Li, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 143 (2021) 12499–12508.
Y. Wang, D. Wang, Y. Li, Adv. Mater. 33 (2021) 2008151.
